2005-07-01.title: British Scientists Say Carbon Dioxide Is Turning the Oceans Acidic
2005-07-01.abstract: The scientists said the growing acidity would be very likely to harm coral reefs and other marine life by the end of the century.

2005-07-01.title: U.S. Freezes Assets of Two Senior Syrian Officials
2005-07-01.abstract: Bush administration orders freeze on any assets in American financial institutions controlled by two senior Syrian intelligence officials who served as Syria's de facto rulers in Lebanon during three-decade occupation that ended in Apr; two men, Ghazi Kanaan and Rustum Ghazali, are pillars of Pres Bashar al-Assad's government, and both are said to have profited enormously from their tenures in Lebanon; step is latest American effort to pressure Syrian government to do more to halt flow of foreign fighters into Iraq; US has already withdrawn its ambassador to Syria (M)

2005-07-01.title: Legacy: A justice who cast the deciding vote

2005-07-02.title: Shorn of subsidies, New Zealand farmers thrive
Clashes in the Georgian Capital Point to Continued Instability
2005-07-02.abstract: The police in Tbilisi, the capital of Georgia, were on alert Friday after an impromptu demonstration led to clashes on Thursday night between protesters and riot police along the city's main avenue.    The leaders of political parties opposed to President Mikheil Saakashvili called for more protests, and they accused Mr. Saakashvili of lurching toward centralized rule. Their calls occurred as the Georgian Parliament erupted in a public fistfight between members of rival political parties arguing in part over an unrelated law.

2005-07-02.title: 10 Russian Commandos Killed by Bombing in Restive Region
2005-07-02.abstract: At least 10 Russian special forces troops were killed on Friday afternoon in the Dagestan region when a bomb exploded outside a public bathhouse in the city of Makhachkala.    The blast wounded 14 other soldiers and 13 civilians, many severely, said Habib Magomedov, a spokesman for the Dagestan Interior Ministry. Mr. Magomedov said all of the troops were members of special units in Russia's Internal Affairs Ministry.

2005-07-02.title: U.S. searches for team missing in Afghanistan
World Briefing | Europe: Britain Takes On European Presidency
2005-07-02.abstract: Tony Blair and Britain took over the rotating presidency of the European Union, and Mr. Blair made it clear that he wants to use his six months at the helm to urge a new economic model in Europe, in effect a call for Germany and France not to rely on social legislation to protect jobs. His first initiative was to propose an informal summit meeting in the fall. ''Everyone in Europe would agree this is an interesting moment to take on the presidency of the European Union, for sure,'' he said.   Graham Bowley (IHT)

2005-07-03.title: For the Poor in Iran, Voting Was About Making Ends Meet
2005-07-03.abstract: They had come from very different neighborhoods and backgrounds, but they were all there for the same reason: to buy government-subsidized food.    The line ran from the basement up the stairs and out the door.

2005-07-03.title: After Centuries of Enmity, Relations Between Poland and Russia Are as Bad as Ever
2005-07-03.abstract: The Poles say, not without a certain pride, that they are the only ones ever to occupy the Kremlin. That was in the early 17th century, almost 200 years before Napoleon and 300 before Hitler failed in their attempts to do so.    In Moscow not long ago, the national day celebration was switched from Nov. 7, commemorating the Bolshevik Revolution, to Nov. 4, when the Russians rid the Kremlin of the hated Poles.

2005-07-03.title: An Afternoon Like America, Cognitive Dissonance and All
2005-07-03.abstract: Just after noon on Saturday, the actor and rapper Will Smith took the stage in front of the Philadelphia Museum of Art, flanked by a copy of the Declaration of Independence and ready to make his own ''declaration of interdependence.''    This was the American installment of the Live 8 concerts, organized to draw attention to African poverty. It seems likely, though, that some (O.K., nearly all) concertgoers were a bit fuzzy on the details and, as the warm but breezy afternoon dragged on, a bit fuzzy on everything else, too.

2005-07-03.title: Egyptian Envoy to Iraq Is Kidnapped in Baghdad
2005-07-03.abstract: The envoy is the most prominent foreign Arab official to be abducted during the war, Iraqi officials said today.

2005-07-04.title: U.S. to join Seoul in new bid to N. Korea
Ukraine workers await word of factories' fate
Schröder and Chirac rebuff Putin on EU growth

2005-07-05.title: Philippine president: Ready to face impeachment
Briefly: Schröder's party seeks ways to win back voters
Rice Plans Asia Trip to Discuss North Korea's Nuclear Program
2005-07-05.abstract: Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice will visit four Asian countries from Friday through the following Wednesday, July 13.

2005-07-05.title: Fireworks as comet takes a hit
Sunni Group Urges Members In Iraq to Vote in the Elections
2005-07-05.abstract: Major Sunni umbrella group, Sunni Endowment, calls on members to register for next round of elections and take part 'despite our reservations'; says clerics will be expected to issue fatwas, or religious rulings, essentially ordering Sunnis to vote in elections; Sunni Endowment is charged with oversight of Sunni Arab mosques and holy sites, giving it wide influence among clerics; next round of voting will include referendum on constitution now being hammered out in National Assembly; Adnan al-Dulaimi, head of Sunni Endowment, explicitly renounces violence as way for Sunnis to regain power; witnesses come forward to describe kidnapping of Ihab al-Sharif, Egypt's top diplomat in Iraq, from Baghdad street; photo (M)

2005-07-06.title: Briefly: Preliminary tally shows opposition leading vote
Attack at Temple in India Leaves 6 Dead; Sectarian Strife Is Feared
2005-07-06.abstract: A brazen attack on India's best-known tinderbox of Hindu-Muslim strife, the heavily fortified Hindu temple compound in the northern town of Ayodhya, left six gunmen dead on Tuesday and raised the specter of a fresh bout of sectarian tension.    After a two-hour firefight in the morning, the police said the temple compound had been secured, six men killed and six weapons, mostly AK-47 assault rifles, recovered. Neither the identities of the men nor their motives have been established.

2005-07-06.title: Bosnian Police Find 2 Bombs Near Memorial
2005-07-06.abstract: The police found two bombs on Tuesday close to the memorial center for the Srebrenica massacre, three days before ceremonies for the 10th anniversary of the atrocities.     The government said in a statement that it did not know whether the bombs were intended to explode and cause injury, or to be safely found in order to cause unrest before the anniversary of the massacre, in which at least 7,000 Muslim men and boys were killed by Bosnian Serb forces.

2005-07-06.title: Schools in Thailand Under Ethnic Siege
2005-07-06.abstract: On the weekends now, the military firing range here is crowded with teachers -- in shifts of 50 -- trying out their pistols, an essential new accessory in a place where teaching school has become one of the most dangerous professions.    In an escalating campaign of violence here in the largely Muslim south of mostly Buddhist Thailand, government-run schools and the teachers who work in them have become particular targets of bombs and gunmen.

2005-07-06.title: Global UN strategy directed at bird flu

2005-07-07.title: Japanese court rejects funds for war orphans
In Africa, a quiet Bush ally
The Scene: Typical Rush Hour Turns Into Chaos
2005-07-07.abstract: A typically drizzly London commute turned into chaos today as passengers were forced onto the streets by a series of explosions.

2005-07-07.title: British Officials Say They Had No Warning
2005-07-07.abstract: Britain's security agencies were focused on security at Gleneagles, Scotland, for the opening of the Group of 8 summit meeting.

2005-07-08.title: Malta becomes 12th nationto support charter for EU
Protection of ground transit lags in the U.S.
4 Blasts, and Blair's Rising Star Runs Into a Treacherous Future
2005-07-08.abstract: Article on Prime Min Tony Blair's 24-hour roller coaster ride from triumph to tragedy, from learning that London would be site of 2012 Olympics and then playing host at Group of 8 summit meeting, to watching horrible television images from London after bombings there; Blair, somber and shaken, flies back to London (M)

2005-07-09.title: Australia grants visa to Chinese diplomat who defected
Envoys make rounds in North Korea crisis
G-8 claims some progress on aid and global warming
For a Hungry Audience, a Japanese Tom Clancy
2005-07-09.abstract: GROPING for a career as he neared 30, Harutoshi Fukui decided to become a novelist while still working full time as a security guard. In little time he quit that job, after winning a prestigious Japanese literary prize in 1998 for ''Twelve Y.O.,'' a political-military thriller whose title recalls Gen. Douglas MacArthur's infamous comment that Japan was a nation of 12-year-olds.    Now 36, Mr. Fukui has to his name a handful of best sellers whose common themes -- postwar Japan's mistaken path, its wounded pride, the sense of stagnation today and the need for a virile military -- resonate in a country shedding its pacifism and rearming itself.

2005-07-09.title: A day when the little things made all the difference

2005-07-10.title: Bishops stop short of asking Arroyo to quit
Iraq suicide bombings kill at least 33
Kyrgyzstan votes to replace leader outsed in uprising
North Koreans Agree to Resume Nuclear Talks
2005-07-10.abstract: After more than a year of stalemate, North Korea agreed Saturday evening to return to disarmament talks late this month and pledged to discuss eliminating its nuclear-weapons program, according to senior Bush administration officials.    The agreement was reached during a dinner meeting, with the Chinese as the hosts, that included Christopher Hill, a former American ambassador to South Korea who has recently become the lead United States negotiator to the talks, and Kim Kye Gwan, North Korea's deputy foreign minister, according to a senior administration official traveling here with Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice.

2005-07-10.title: North Korea agrees to rejoin nuclear talks

2005-07-11.title: Bush, not Africa, wins most from G-8
Visiting Tsunami Survivors, Rice Faces Annoyed Asian Leaders
2005-07-11.abstract: The secretary of state flew into a storm of criticism over her decision not to attend an annual Southeast Asian summit.

2005-07-11.title: Taliban Kill 10 Afghan Police Officers
2005-07-11.abstract: Ten Afghan border police officers were killed in a clash with Taliban fighters in the country's south, and all are believed to have been beheaded, government officials said Sunday.     The killings took place Thursday afternoon, when border police forces came under attack in Helmand Province, roughly 400 miles south of Kabul, and were unable to flee because their vehicle became bogged down in sand, said Ali Ahmad Jalali, the Afghan interior minister. Four were killed immediately, the minister said. The bodies of the six others were left 200 yards from the Pakistani border, he said. The attackers then fled across the border.

2005-07-11.title: With No Leads, British Consult Allies on Blasts
2005-07-11.abstract: British intelligence officials summon counterparts in United States and two dozen European allies to help in developing possible leads in worst terrorist attack in Britain since World War II; Britain is regarded by other European countries as often having access to more and better quality of intelligence because it is part of long-established, Anglophone intelligence-sharing agreement with US, Canada, Australia and New Zealand; but European participants in meeting voice surprise at how little is known about these attacks, which hit three trains in London Underground and double-decker bus; unusual meeting indicates that British officials running complex inquiry are frustrated because they have had few breaks, few leads and no suspects in first 48 hours after attack, most important investigative period after any terrorist bombing (M)

2005-07-12.title: At least one bomber died in London attacks, police say
World Briefing | Africa: Somalia: Peace Activist Gunned Down
2005-07-12.abstract: Gunmen killed Abdulkadir Yahya Ali, a prominent peace activist, at his home in Mogadishu in the latest in a string of targeted attacks on civilians who are seeking to promote stability in Somalia. Mr. Yahya founded the Center for Research and Dialogue, a group that has worked to help reconstruct the war-torn country. ''This is a great loss to Mogadishu and Somalia at this particular time when people of his courage and tenacity are most needed,'' said Maxwell Gaylard, the United Nation's humanitarian coordinator for Somalia.   Marc Lacey (NYT)

2005-07-12.title: South Korea Offers a Trade for North's Disarmament
2005-07-12.abstract: South Korea offered to supply North Korea with electrical power, should the north agree to dismantle its nuclear-arms program.

2005-07-13.title: Italy Proposes New Powers to Counter Terrorists
2005-07-13.abstract: Warning that ''terrorism is knocking on Italy's door'' in the wake of the London bombings, Italy's interior minister went before Parliament on Tuesday to request new security powers that he said would help combat the threat.    The interior minister, Giuseppe Pisanu, proposed that security forces be allowed to hold terrorism suspects for 24 hours without charges, instead of the current 12 hours, and that the government be allowed to archive private e-mail communications for prolonged periods.

2005-07-13.title: World Briefing | Europe: European Union: Joining Requires Reform
2005-07-13.abstract: Olli Rehn, the European Union's top official responsible for enlargement, issued a blunt warning to Bulgaria and Romania, telling them that their membership in the European Union in 2007 would be postponed if they did not carry out necessary reforms. He cited the need to address corruption and to reform the judiciary in both countries. Mr. Rehn also said that member states should not close the door on including Turkey, despite increasing skepticism among Europeans toward enlargement.   Judy Dempsey (IHT)

2005-07-13.title: For soccer's stars, check is reality
South Korea Offers Power if North Quits Arms Program
2005-07-13.abstract: South Korea offers to provide North Korea with vast supply of electical power if it dismantles nuclear-weapons program; US may incorporate proposal into offer made in six-party talks in 2004; South Korean official Chung Dong Young explains offer, which he has presented to Kim Jong Il (M)

2005-07-13.title: Filipino parties back charter reform to end crisis

2005-07-14.title: As Police Search for Clues, Britain Pauses to Mourn
2005-07-14.abstract: London held a moment of silence for its victims, now estimated to number 53, and police identified one of the suspects.

2005-07-14.title: Top Officials in Europe Vow to Press Fight on Terrorism
2005-07-14.abstract: Interior and justice ministers of 25 European Union countries hold special meeting in Brussels and renew pledges of concerted action against terrorism (M)

2005-07-14.title: Data Shows Rising Toll of Iraqis From Insurgency
2005-07-14.abstract: Iraqi Interior Ministry reports that 8,175 Iraqi civilians and police officers were killed by insurgents from August 2004 through May 31, rate of more than 800 a month; figures do not include Iraqi soldiers or civilians killed during US military operations; US has avoided Iraqi body count, but estimates of Iraqi dead range from official estimate of 12,000 in June to 100,000 in widely reported study last year; second set of Iraqi figures shows highest percentage of deaths in Baghdad, second highest in Anbar, largely Sunni province; photos; map; chart (M)

2005-07-14.title: Under heavy security, France celebrates Bastille Day

2005-07-15.title: Italy won't tighten controls at borders
1 million expected to voteon leader of Kuomintang
Reactions from around the world to the Pew findings
Aceh peace talks stall as Jakarta opposes rebel political ambitions

2005-07-16.title: World Briefing | Americas: Venezuela: Protest Against Cuban Doctors
2005-07-16.abstract: Hundreds of doctors protested in Caracas, saying their jobs were in danger because of a government program that has brought in 20,000 Cuban doctors and dentists. The Cuban doctors are popular in poor neighborhoods, where health care had been rare, but Venezuelan medical workers say that the Cubans are unqualified and that the program that brought them to Venezuela siphons off money for medical equipment. President Hugo Chávez has close ties with the Cuban government, which receives cut-rate oil shipments in return for sending medical teams to Venezuela.   Juan Forero (NYT)

2005-07-16.title: World Briefing | Asia: Indonesia: Bird Flu Suspected In 3 Deaths
2005-07-16.abstract: Indonesian health officials said the bird flu virus was the probable cause of the deaths of a 38-year-old man and his two daughters. If confirmed, these would be the first people to have been killed by bird flu in Indonesia. Blood samples have tested positive for the virus, but further tests will be conducted. Officials also said they had no evidence that the victims had any contact with poultry, raising the possibility of human-to-human transmission of the virus.   Evelyn Rusli (NYT)

2005-07-16.title: Anger Burns on the Fringe of Britain's Muslims
2005-07-16.abstract: At Beeston's Cross Flats Park, in the center of this now embattled town, Sanjay Dutt and his friends grappled Friday with why their friend Kakey, better known to the world as Shehzad Tanweer, had decided to become a suicide bomber.     ''He was sick of it all, all the injustice and the way the world is going about it,'' Mr. Dutt, 22, said. ''Why, for example, don't they ever take a moment of silence for all the Iraqi kids who die?''

2005-07-16.title: U.S. Forces Kill 24 Militants Fleeing to Pakistan
2005-07-16.abstract: American troops killed 24 men suspected of being Taliban fighters fleeing Afghanistan into Pakistani territory, the Pakistani military said Friday, but left unclear whether American forces had crossed into Pakistani territory.    Maj. Gen. Shaukat Sultan, the Pakistani Army spokesman, said in a telephone interview from Islamabad that American-led coalition forces attacked the suspects on Thursday in the eastern Khost region of Afghanistan, prompting them to flee across the border. ''They were hit by coalition forces on the Pakistan side, when they were trying to run away from the operation,'' General Sultan said.

2005-07-17.title: Aceh rebel's military wing welcomes draft peace deal
By Courting Sunnis, G.I.'s See Security Rise in a Sinister Town
2005-07-17.abstract: These days, this town in northern Iraq is relatively safe compared with other Sunni Arab-dominated areas of Iraq. For a village that long held folk status among Iraqis as one of the most sinister places in the country, that is quite a change.    As the insurgency gathered strength last year, Qabr Abed served as a weapons depot and safe haven for an unusually large number of home-grown insurgent commanders, including Mohammed Shakara, Al Qaeda's leader for northern Iraq and its biggest city, Mosul.

2005-07-17.title: China hails new leadern of Taiwan Nationalists
In a Show of Force, Israeli Forces Gather Outside the Gaza Strip
2005-07-17.abstract: Ariel Sharon told his cabinet ministers there would be "no restriction on our activities to halt the attacks at communities both inside and outside the Gaza Strip."

2005-07-17.title: China Hails New Leader of Taiwan Nationalists
2005-07-17.abstract: BEIJING, July 17 - The Chinese president, Hu Jintao, hailed today the election of Ma Ying-jeou, the mayor of Taipei, as the chairman of Taiwan's main opposition party, signaling Beijing's expectation that the new party leader will continue to try to improve relations across the Taiwan Strait.

2005-07-18.title: Indian Leader Is Warmly Welcomed at White House
2005-07-18.abstract: President Bush and Prime Minister Manmohan Singh pledged closer ties, but failed to settle some key issues.

2005-07-18.title: French leak stirs fury in Britain
Pakistan Kills 17 Fighting Militants Near Afghanistan
2005-07-18.abstract: Pakistani military spokesman Maj Gen Shaukat Sultan reports that 17 people were killed during military operation against militants in North Waziristan Province near border with Afghanistan; asserts that some of the dead and injured were women and children used as human shields; photo (S)

2005-07-18.title: Sordid Images on TV Repel the Kurds, but They Raise Some Skepticism, Too
2005-07-18.abstract: Authorities in Erbil, northern Iraq Kurdish stronghold, release videotaped confession of Sheik Zana, local shopkeeper and mechanical engineer who admits to host of atrocities including murder and rape; videos have been distributed to local television stations and are intended to expose terrorist tactics and turn recruits away from joining militant movement; graphic scenes depict beheadings, mutilation, and homosexual sex; critics says videotapes are staged; viewers describe their reaction to violence; photos (M)

2005-07-19.title: Names of the Dead
2005-07-19.abstract: Defense Department confirms deaths of seven service members in Iraq, bringing to 1,759 number who have died since start of war

2005-07-19.title: U.S. to Broaden India's Access To Nuclear-Power Technology
2005-07-19.abstract: Pres Bush reaches agreement with Prime Min Manmohan Singh for India to secure international help for its civilian nuclear reactors while retaining its nuclear arms; photos of two at White House; agreement, if approved by other nuclear countries and Congress, would remove ban on civilian nuclear technology sales to India that has been decades-long source of antagonism; India would obtain nuclear fuel and reactor components and in return allow international inspections and refrain from further weapons testing and arms transfers; Pakistan is likely to demand similar concessions; agreement is major step in administration campaign to improve ties (M)

2005-07-19.title: In libel case, Polanski denies seduction
June Report Led Britain to Lower Its Terror Alert
2005-07-19.abstract: British intelligence and law enforcement officials reportedly concluded in June, less than month before London terrorist bombings, that 'at present there is not a group with both the current intent and capability' of attacking country; confidential threat assessment prompted government to lower formal threat level from 'severe defined' to 'substantial,' although officials say reduced threat level had no practical impact on preventive measures; photo; assessment reportedly found terrorist activity in Britain to be direct result of Iraq war, linkage sharply criticized by Prime Min Tony Blair and other officials since London bombings; report by Royal Institute of International Affairs says British role in Iraq war has made it vulnerable to attack; police admit potential suspect, on watch list, entered and left Britain in period before bombings but was not under surveillance; also questioned is why one of four bombers, Mohammad Sidique Khan, was not deemed threat in 2004, when he reportedly was investigated in plot to build bomb (M)

2005-07-19.title: Gunmen Kill 2 Sunnis on Iraqi Constitution Panel
2005-07-19.abstract: The killings could jeopardize efforts to draw more Sunni Arabs into the democratic process.

2005-07-20.title: Colleagues call high court nominee a smart, self-effacing 'Eagle Scout'
Senate moving quickly to try to fill Supreme Court opening by October
Bush names John G. Roberts Jr. to Supreme Court
Car bomb in Indian Kashmir kills at least six
2 Sunnis at Work on Constitution Are Shot Dead in Car in Baghdad
2005-07-20.abstract: Two Sunni Arabs involved in drafting Iraq's constitution are assassinated on busy street in central Baghdad; bodyguard also killed; Mejbil al-Sheik Isa was among 15 Sunnis recently named to committee and Damin al-Obeidi was one of ten Sunni consultants; killings raise questions about efforts to draw in more Sunnis to make process more inclusive; Sunnis and Shiites alike condemn killings, as does UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan; photos of scene and of women's groups demonstrating against constitutional draft that would strengthen Islamic law regarding women and families; attacks come as Pres Jalal Talabani predicts constitution could be completed by end of month; more than dozen people are killed in other attacks (M)

2005-07-21.title: India welcomed as new sort of superpower
Polanski trial hears witness on advances
Londoners Carry On Despite Chaos and Confusion
2005-07-21.abstract: LONDON, July 21 - The rumors swirled around the city like poison gas. Smoke was billowing from three subway stations. A bus had exploded. There were chemical weapons, nail bombs, gas attacks. The London Underground had been put on

2005-07-22.title: Police search for bombers after new blasts in London
Hussein Jousts With Iraqi Judge Over His Rights in a Court Hearing
2005-07-22.abstract: Videotape of one of Saddam Hussein's court appearances is aired on Dubai-based Al Arabiya television; Hussein appears vocally weaker and visibly thinner than in other tapes released; spars with Judge Munir Hadad over his rights to see his attorney and questions court's overall authority; will be tried for several crimes, but court will begin with his involvement in destruction and murder at Dujail in 1982; photo (M)

2005-07-22.title: Indian water case pits village against a giant
Names of the Dead
2005-07-22.abstract: Defense Department confirms death of one service member in Iraq (S)

2005-07-23.title: World Briefing | Africa: Nepal: Peacekeepers Sentenced To Prison
2005-07-23.abstract: The Royal Nepal Army said a military court had sentenced six soldiers to three months in prison for sex abuses committed a few months ago while deployed as United Nations peacekeepers in Congo, the New China News Agency reported. Three of the soldiers were also demoted, said Brig. Gen. Deepak Gurung, an army spokesman.

2005-07-23.title: Bombing Suspect Killed in Subway by London Police
2005-07-23.abstract: Plainclothes police officers on Friday fatally shot a suspect on a subway train as horrified passengers looked on.

2005-07-24.title: Myanmar likely to forego key Asean chairmanship
From Shoes to Aircraft to Investment, Zimbabwe Pursues a Made-in-China Future
2005-07-24.abstract: His new 25-bedroom palace is clad in midnight-blue Chinese roof tiles. His air force trains on Chinese jets. His subjects wear Chinese shoes, ride Chinese buses and, lately, zip around the country in Chinese propjets. He has even urged his countrymen to learn Mandarin and nurture a taste for Chinese cuisine.    That President Robert G. Mugabe rules Zimbabwe, which resembles China about as much as African corn porridge tastes like moo shu pork, is irrelevant. Tightening his embrace of all things Chinese, the 81-year-old Mr. Mugabe, Zimbabwe's canny autocrat for 25 years, arrived in Beijing on Saturday for six days of talks with China's leaders, led by President Hu Jintao.

2005-07-24.title: U.S. Sees Progress and Hope for Accord in Gaza Talks
2005-07-24.abstract: With just 24 days to go before Israel begins withdrawing from the Gaza Strip, American officials acknowledge that none of the major outstanding issues that all sides agree need to be settled so that Gaza does not effectively become a prison for its 1.3 million residents have been resolved.    Secretary of State Condoleezza Rice finished two days of mediation between Israelis and Palestinians on Saturday evening, and officials traveling with her said they might have made incremental progress on some issues but no breakthroughs.

2005-07-24.title: Journalist Jailed for a Year in Kabul Feels Abandoned by U.S. as He Seeks Ways to Survive
2005-07-24.abstract: On visitors day at Kabul's once-notorious Pul-i-Charkhi prison, there is one inmate who stands out from the Afghans, wearing blue shades and an American T-shirt. It is Edward Caraballo, 43, an independent filmmaker from the Bronx, who was one of the three Americans found guilty last year by a Kabul court of running a private jail and torturing hostages.     ''I usually wear Afghan clothes,'' he said during the visit earlier this month, referring to the baggy shirt and pants that is the local dress. ''But today is the Fourth of July, so I put on my T-shirt in honor of my country.''

2005-07-24.title: Alain Bombard, 80, Dies; Sailed the Atlantic Alone
2005-07-24.abstract: Alain Bombard, who deliberately drifted across the Atlantic for 65 days in a lifeboat with no provisions -- subsisting on plankton, saltwater and raw fish -- to prove it was possible, died on July 19 at a hospital in Toulon, France. He was 80.     His death was reported by French, English and American news agencies, none of which gave a cause.

2005-07-25.title: Family of slain Brazilian say they may sue British police
In Most Cases, Israel Thwarts Suicide Attacks Without a Shot
2005-07-25.abstract: Capture of would-be suicide bomber by Israeli military is sharp contrast to killing of innocent man by British police, who mistakenly thought he was suicide bomber; Israelis hae failed to stop suicide bombers many times, but they have succeeded far more often; description of differences in situations faced by two countries (M)

2005-07-25.title: News analysis: Policing a changed landscape in London
U.S., North Korean envoys meet before nuclear talks
Arroyo Seeks to Shore Up Plunging Popularity
2005-07-25.abstract: The president of the Philippines urged Congress to accelerate a shift to a parliamentary form of government.

2005-07-26.title: World Briefing | Europe: Russia: Ex-Premier Denounces 'Smear Campaign'
2005-07-26.abstract: Former Russian Prime Min Mikhail M Kasyanov denounces recent reports of financial impropriety; says that allegations, which were launched shortly after he suggested he might run for president in 2008, will not deter him from political pursuits; photo (S)

2005-07-26.title: Rumsfeld Stop in Kyrgyzstan Aims to Keep Access to Base
2005-07-26.abstract: Defense Secretary Donald H Rumsfeld arrives at Manas Air Base in Kyrgzstan where US forces operate refueling and cargo mission for civilians, troops and equipment going in and out of Afghanistan; Shanghai Cooperation Organization, group of nations which is dominated by Russia and China, has called for US military pullout from region; Rumsfeld, on three-day trip to Central Asia, stresses importance of military installations in region; some speculate Rumsfeld is looking for alternative bases if troops are forced to move; photo (M)

2005-07-26.title: 12 Die in Iraq Bus Attacks; Kidnappers Threaten to Kill Envoys in Video
2005-07-26.abstract: A group linked with Abu Musab al-Zarqawi released a videotape of two kidnapped Algerian diplomats and threatened to kill them.

2005-07-26.title: News Analysis:Asean face 'difficult position' on Myanmar
Pakistani involvement in Egypt attack ruled out

2005-07-27.title: Record rains kill at least 71 in India
World Briefing | United Nations: Security Council Votes To Monitor Child Soldiers
2005-07-27.abstract: United Nations Security Council adopts resolution that will impose sanctions on any nation or group that kidnaps or abuses children or enlists them as soldiers in their cause; estimates that in past ten years two million children were killed and six million injured or disabled worldwide in insurgent conflicts (S)

2005-07-27.title: 3 Main British Parties to Back Tougher Antiterrorism Laws
2005-07-27.abstract: Prime Min Tony Blair promises not to 'give one inch' on British policies in Middle East and says Conservatives and Liberal Democrats are moving closer to agreement on new antiterrorism legislation; condemns as 'complete nonsense' argument that Iraq war led to bombings in London, saying there is not sliver of justification; also defends police shooting of Brazilian man mistakenly feared to be suicide bomber; subway operator reveals that he heard shots, ran through tunnel and says he was almost shot by officers himself (M)

2005-07-27.title: In Surprise Visit to Baghdad, Rumsfeld Prods Iraqi Leaders
2005-07-27.abstract: The defense secretary laid out a remarkably blunt prescription for what Iraqi leaders must do to ensure a stable government and to allow the U.S. to begin to withdraw.

2005-07-27.title: World Briefing | The Americas: Mexico: Judge Throws Out Genocide Charge
2005-07-27.abstract: Mexican Judge Herlinda Valasco Villavicencio throws out charges of genocide levied against former Pres Luis Echeverria and his interior minister; former politicians were charged with killing political dissidents in late 1960's and early 1970's (S)

2005-07-28.title: World Briefing: Americas; Africa; Europe; Middle East
Heavy Prison Terms in France for 62 Pedophiles
2005-07-28.abstract: French court hands down long sentences in pedophilia case involving 65 adults accused of abusing 45 children in Angers in central France; case involves 39 men and 26 women charged with rape or selling their children for sex (M)

2005-07-28.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Local Police Return To Streets Of Violent Border City
2005-07-28.abstract: A month after federal agents seized control of Nuevo Laredo, the city's police officers again began patrolling its violent streets in new uniforms and patrol cars. A third of the force has been fired for corruption and links to drug trafficking, but the drug-related shootings that have plagued the city for months have continued. At least 39 people have been killed since the federal takeover last month, which followed the new police chief's killing and the local police opening fire on a group of federal agents sent in to restore order. The violence has caused the United States to issue three warnings just this year to tourists thinking of traveling to border cities.   James C. McKinley Jr. (NYT)

2005-07-28.title: South Korean envoy to U.S. resigns amid allegations
Briefly: By one vote, Socialist is chosen as leader

2005-07-29.title: Briefly: 6 nations create group for cleaner technology
Fire linked to arsonists hits suburbs of Athens
What's Doing in France: Hip Theater, Heartily Booed
2005-07-29.abstract: If this southern French city had a motto, it might be, ''Say what you like about me, just make sure you spell my name right.''      This summer, the annual theater festival in Avignon, arguably the best known in France, appeared to be putting that age-old publicity strategy to the test.

2005-07-29.title: World Briefing | Africa: Uganda: Multiparty Referendum Ignored
2005-07-29.abstract: Voters went to the polls in low numbers to decide whether to allow multiparty politics after nearly 20 years. Opposition leaders had declared the referendum a waste of time, and many Ugandans regarded the change preordained since President Yoweri Museveni had endorsed it. His party has governed since seizing power in 1986, and Parliament recently altered the Constitution to allow him to run for a third term.   Marc Lacey (NYT)

2005-07-29.title: Mubarak adds Western touch to election bid

2005-07-30.title: Slain Brazilian is buried amid quiet anger
Taiwan signals easing of travel ban
World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: Mayor Resigns To Seek Presidency
2005-07-30.abstract: Mayor Andrés Manuel López Obrador of Mexico City, the leader of the leftist Democratic Revolutionary Party, stepped down to begin his campaign for president in next year's election. Polls continue to show him to be the most popular politician in the country.   Ginger Thompson (NYT)

2005-07-30.title: World Briefing | Americas: Mexico: U.S. Shuts Consulate In Lawless City
2005-07-30.abstract: A day after rival drug gangs clashed on a residential street in Nuevo Laredo with automatic weapons, grenades and bazookas, the American ambassador to Mexico, Tony Garza, said he was ordering the United States Consulate there closed for a week and would open it again only if the security situation improved. No deaths were reported in the latest gun battle in the city, across the Rio Grande from Laredo, Tex., but it came just days after the State Department issued its third travel warning in less than a year over drug-related violence along Mexico's northern border, with Mr. Garza saying that there had been more than 100 violent deaths there since just last month.   Antonio Betancourt (NYT)

2005-07-30.title: To Some in Ulster, Celebration of I.R.A. Pledge Is Early
2005-07-30.abstract: If Northern Ireland woke up on Friday morning to a new era in the province's troubled history, few people here were in a mood to celebrate.      The Irish Republican Army's pledge to end its bloody 36-year campaign of violence, splashed across Belfast's dozen morning newspapers, did not really change anything, some residents said. Large-scale bombings and attacks mainly stopped with the group's 1994 cease-fire, they said, but low-level violence and organized crime have continued.

2005-07-31.title: Bomb Kills 2 Private Guards for British Consulate in Iraq
2005-07-31.abstract: Two British security contractors working for the British consulate in the southern city of Basra were killed Saturday by a roadside bomb while driving in a convoy of vehicles on the outskirts of the city, police and consular officials reported.      The deaths were the first among the consulate's staff, according to Karen McLuskie, a consular spokeswoman, and challenged the widely held view of Basra, Iraq's second largest city, as an oasis of calm compared with the violent tumult of Baghdad and other cities.

2005-07-31.title: Two Koreas agree to open connecting railways, roads in October
New Iran Leader to Address U.N.
2005-07-31.abstract: Iran's incoming conservative president, Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, is planning to address the United Nations General Assembly in September on his first official trip abroad despite investigations by the United States about whether he took part in the siege at the American Embassy in Tehran in 1979.     Iran and the United States severed diplomatic ties after students took diplomats, military officers and others hostage. Mr. Ahmadinejad has denied accusations by some former hostages that he took part. As the head of the government of a United Nations member state, he will have diplomatic immunity on his trip.

2005-07-31.title: Robert Maguire, 75, Expert on Soviet Literature, Dies
2005-07-31.abstract: Robert Alan Maguire, an authority on early Soviet literature and the writings of Nikolai Gogol, died on July 8 at Calvary Hospital in the Bronx. He was 75 and lived in Morningside Heights in Manhattan.     The cause of death was cancer, according to Columbia University, where he was the Boris Bakhmeteff professor emeritus in the Department of Slavic Languages.

2005-07-31.title: Hundreds of protesters take to the streets in northern Sinai
